title: Global Foundry 2.0 Revenue Rises 23% YoY in Q1 2026 as AI Continues to Boost Demand
gist: Counterpoint Research's Foundry 2.0 tracker shows global foundry-ecosystem revenue up 23% YoY to $86 billion in Q1 2026, led by TSMC's 41% YoY growth (36% guided for full-year 2026), with AI-driven demand now extending beyond wafer fabrication into a packaging/testing bottleneck at OSATs (ASE +18%, Amkor +25%) and creating overflow opportunities for Intel Foundry, Samsung Foundry, UMC, and Vanguard.

themes.theme: Foundry 2.0: AI cycle reshapes the whole manufacturing value chain

insights.claim: The global Foundry 2.0 market's revenue grew 23% YoY to $86 billion in Q1 2026, driven by AI GPU and AI ASIC demand across both leading-edge process nodes and advanced packaging, per Counterpoint Research's Foundry Market Supply Tracker.
insights.speaker: Counterpoint Research
insights.so_what: Headline figure ($86B, +23% YoY) sizes the current scale of the AI-driven foundry upcycle across the whole ecosystem, not just TSMC — a top-level benchmark for tracking whether AI capex is translating into realized foundry revenue.
insights.quote: The global foundry 2.0 market revenue climbed 23% YoY in Q1 2026 to reach $86 billion, according to Counterpoint Research’s latest Foundry Market Supply Tracker.

insights.claim: Counterpoint's 'Foundry 2.0' framework expands the traditional pure-play chip manufacturing definition to include non-memory IDMs, OSATs, and photomask suppliers, reflecting a structural shift toward closer alignment across design, manufacturing, and packaging to improve system-level efficiency and total cost of ownership.
insights.speaker: Counterpoint Research
insights.so_what: A named analytical framework (Foundry 2.0) investors can adopt to value the full manufacturing ecosystem — including OSATs and photomask makers — as strategically as pure-play foundries, since packaging is now a co-equal bottleneck.
insights.quote: Counterpoint’s “Foundry 2.0” definition expands the scope to include pure-play foundries, non-memory Integrated Device Manufacturers (IDMs), Outsourced Semiconductor Assembly and Test (OSAT) companies and photomask suppliers.

insights.claim: Competitive advantage in the current AI cycle is no longer determined solely by process technology, but by the ability to deliver advanced packaging and foundry capacity at scale, as AI systems become more complex and packaging-intensive.
insights.speaker: Counterpoint Research
insights.so_what: Reframes the competitive battleground in semiconductors from node leadership alone to combined node-plus-packaging capacity — relevant for evaluating which foundries/OSATs will capture AI-driven share gains.
insights.quote: As AI systems become more complex and packaging-intensive, competitive advantages are no longer determined solely by process technology, but by the ability to deliver advanced packaging and foundry capacity at scale.
insights.locator: paragraph after Foundry 2.0 Market Revenue Share chart

themes.theme: TSMC's outsized share of the AI upcycle

insights.claim: TSMC's revenue growth accelerated to 41% YoY in Q1 2026, and Counterpoint expects TSMC to deliver about 36% YoY revenue growth for full-year 2026, driven by AI GPU, AI ASIC, and advanced packaging demand.
insights.speaker: William Li (Counterpoint Research)
insights.so_what: Concrete growth benchmarks (41% Q1 YoY, 36% full-year 2026 guide) for TSMC investors to track against actual quarterly results as the AI cycle progresses.
insights.quote: TSMC remained the primary beneficiary of the AI-driven semiconductor upcycle, with revenue growth accelerating to 41% YoY in Q1 2026.
insights.locator: Pure Play Foundries Revenue Growth section

insights.claim: TSMC is undertaking unprecedented capacity reallocations across multiple fabs, converting mature-node capacity to support advanced technologies, and has adopted pricing actions that differ from its traditional annual pricing framework, reflecting demand intensity beyond previous semiconductor cycles.
insights.speaker: William Li (Counterpoint Research)
insights.so_what: TSMC breaking from its traditional annual pricing cadence signals unusual pricing power and demand tightness — a material signal for modeling TSMC's near-term margin trajectory and customer negotiating leverage.
insights.quote: TSMC has adopted pricing actions that differ from its traditional annual pricing framework, highlighting a level of demand intensity in previous semiconductor cycles.

insights.claim: Combined with continued CoWoS capacity constraints, the current AI boom reflects more than a cyclical upturn — it points to a broader structural transformation across the semiconductor industry.
insights.speaker: William Li (Counterpoint Research)
insights.so_what: An explicit analyst call that the current AI-driven upcycle is structural, not cyclical — a thesis investors should weigh when deciding whether to treat current foundry/packaging demand levels as durable versus mean-reverting.
insights.quote: we believe the current AI boom reflects more than just a cyclical upturn. It points to a broader structural transformation across the semiconductor industry.

insights.claim: Non-TSMC pure-play foundries reported a modest 9% YoY revenue increase in Q1 2026, with Chinese foundries benefiting from domestic localization demand and structural wafer price increases: SMIC rose 12% YoY and Nexchip rose 19% YoY.
insights.speaker: Counterpoint Research
insights.so_what: Quantifies the growth gap between TSMC (41% YoY) and the rest of the pure-play foundry field (9% YoY average), showing AI-driven demand is heavily concentrated at the leading edge while Chinese foundries grow on separate localization dynamics.
insights.quote: Non-TSMC pure-play foundries also reported a modest 9% YoY revenue increase in Q1 2026... SMIC’s revenue rose 12% YoY, while Nexchip achieved a 19% YoY increase.

insights.claim: UMC's revenue rose 10% YoY and Vanguard's rose 14% YoY in Q1 2026, benefiting from recovering consumer electronics demand and PMIC strength, and both are assessed as well positioned to capture TSMC's overflow orders as TSMC optimizes mature-node capacity.
insights.speaker: Counterpoint Research
insights.so_what: Identifies UMC and Vanguard as direct beneficiaries of TSMC capacity reallocation away from mature nodes — a specific overflow-demand thesis to track in these companies' order books.
insights.quote: Counterpoint Research has assessed that both foundries are well positioned to capture TSMC’s overflow orders as the company is optimizing its capacity at mature nodes.

insights.claim: MediaTek securing a larger share of Google TPUs, along with other potential ASIC opportunities, could become an important driver of wafer demand, and as such orders scale, capacity constraints across both advanced nodes and advanced packaging will likely escalate, creating incremental opportunities for other foundry suppliers including Intel Foundry and Samsung Foundry.
insights.speaker: Counterpoint Research
insights.so_what: Flags MediaTek's Google TPU business as a wafer-demand driver that could push overflow business to Intel Foundry and Samsung Foundry — a specific customer/product relationship worth tracking for foundry capacity allocation shifts.
insights.quote: Meanwhile, MediaTek securing a larger share of Google TPUs, along with other potential ASIC opportunities, could likely become an important driver of wafer demand.

insights.claim: A potential Apple M7 design win on Intel 18A-P would further solidify market confidence in Intel Foundry, as growing TPU/ASIC demand tightens leading-edge capacity and creates opportunities for Intel Foundry and Samsung Foundry.
insights.speaker: Counterpoint Research
insights.so_what: Names a specific, high-visibility potential catalyst (Apple M7 on Intel 18A-P) for Intel Foundry's turnaround narrative — a concrete milestone for investors to watch for confirmation.
insights.quote: Potential design wins, such as Apple M7 on Intel 18A-P, would further solidify market confidence in Intel Foundry.
insights.locator: paragraph on Intel Foundry and Samsung Foundry

themes.theme: OSAT/packaging emerges as the new AI supply chain bottleneck

insights.claim: ASE reported 18% YoY revenue growth in Q1 2026 and raised its LEAP advanced packaging revenue target to over $3.5 billion for 2026, up from the prior quarter's $3.2 billion guidance.
insights.speaker: Counterpoint Research
insights.so_what: ASE's raised advanced-packaging guidance ($3.2B to $3.5B+) is a direct, trackable indicator of tightening CoWoS/advanced packaging demand flowing from AI GPU/ASIC production.
insights.quote: ASE reported 18% YoY revenue growth and raised its LEAP advanced packaging revenue target to over $3.5 billion for 2026, from the $3.2 billion guidance in the last quarter.

insights.claim: Amkor's revenue jumped 25% YoY to a record high on high advanced-packaging utilization rates, and management expects AI-related revenue to triple in 2026, supported by its Arizona ramp in H2 2026.
insights.speaker: Counterpoint Research
insights.so_what: A tripling of AI-related revenue guidance for Amkor in 2026, tied to a specific US (Arizona) capacity ramp, is a concrete catalyst for tracking US-based advanced packaging capacity coming online.
insights.quote: Amkor’s revenue jumped 25% YoY to hit a record high, thanks to high utilization rates across advanced packaging lines. Management expects AI-related revenue to triple in 2026, supported by its Arizona ramp in H2 2026.

insights.claim: Advanced packaging has become a key bottleneck in AI deployment, with OSAT vendors seeing better visibility and tighter demand, evidenced by Tongfu's 29% YoY growth (driven by AMD's AI packaging ramp), KYEC's 45% YoY growth (longer AI test lead times), and Powertech capturing spillover demand while moving into FOPLP technology.
insights.speaker: Brady Wang (Counterpoint Research)
insights.so_what: Names specific second-tier OSAT beneficiaries (Tongfu tied to AMD, KYEC on test lead times, Powertech on FOPLP) of the AI packaging bottleneck — a broader basket of names beyond ASE/Amkor for tracking AI supply chain exposure.
insights.quote: Advanced packaging has become a key bottleneck in AI deployment. OSAT vendors now benefit from better visibility and tighter demand, supported by robust Q1 2026 results and aggressive capacity expansion across the ecosystem.

themes.theme: Non-memory IDMs riding AI/data-center power demand recovery

insights.claim: Non-memory IDMs continued to recover in Q1 2026, benefiting from improving industrial demand and growing exposure to AI/data-center power management, led by STMicroelectronics' 21% YoY revenue growth; the sector's recovery is expected to gain further momentum in H2 2026.
insights.speaker: Counterpoint Research
insights.so_what: Signals AI-driven power-management chip demand is now lifting non-memory IDMs (not just leading-edge foundries/OSATs), broadening the set of AI-cycle beneficiaries to include analog/power semiconductor names like STMicroelectronics.
insights.quote: Most companies within the group reported double-digit percentage YoY revenue growth, led by STMicroelectronics, which delivered a strong 21% YoY rise.
